evidence_quotes.quote: These companies risk withdrawing payment services from platforms if they fail to comply with their requests.
Payment companies should not have the power to decide what customers can buy for their money if the content is legal.
Such conduct by payment companies is indicative of monopolistic behaviour and an intolerable interference with the freedom of the market.

paragraphs.text: I would like to draw attention to the worrying practices of payment companies, such as Visa and Mastercard, which put pressure on platforms such as Steam or Itch.io to remove products from their offers and even from customer accounts, which they consider inappropriate, even though they are perfectly legal in many countries. These companies risk withdrawing payment services from platforms if they fail to comply with their requests. This pressure was triggered by the Australian Collective SHOUT campaign, which advocated the removal of certain content. It is worrying that the demands of a small group from one country may lead to global media censorship. Moreover, this group openly opposes abortion rights and the LGBTQ+ community, raising further questions about their motivation. Such conduct by payment companies is indicative of monopolistic behaviour and an intolerable interference with the freedom of the market. While this was mostly adult content, in the future platforms could be forced to remove LGBTQ+ content, politically critical materials or any other content that would be considered problematic by a group. Payment companies should not have the power to decide what customers can buy for their money if the content is legal. This precedent threatens individual and business freedom and could lead to further censorship. Please consider this issue as a warning against the danger of monopolistic influence and excessive interference in legal trade.
